mn5:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
mn5:1.3: There the Venerable Sāriputta addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn5:1.5: “Friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to the Venerable Sāriputta.
mn5:10.1: “Now, friend, it is possible that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:10.2: ‘If only I had committed an offense, but the *bhikkhus* would not know that I had committed an offense.’
mn5:10.3: But it is possible, friend, that the *bhikkhus* would know of that *bhikkhu*:
mn5:10.5: ‘The *bhikkhus* know that I have committed an offense’—
mn5:10.6: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:10.7: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:10.8: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:11.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:11.2: ‘If only I had committed an offense, and the *bhikkhus* would reprove me privately, not in the midst of the *saṅgha*.’
mn5:11.3: But it is possible, friend, that the *bhikkhus* would reprove that *bhikkhu* in the midst of the *saṅgha*, not privately.
mn5:11.4: ‘The *bhikkhus* reprove me in the midst of the *saṅgha*, not privately’—
mn5:11.5: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:11.6: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:11.7: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:12.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:12.2: ‘If only I had committed an offense, and one who is my equal would reprove me, not one who is not my equal.’
mn5:12.3: But it is possible, friend, that one who is not his equal would reprove that *bhikkhu*, not one who is his equal.
mn5:12.4: ‘One who is not my equal reproves me, not one who is my equal’—
mn5:12.5: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:12.6: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:12.7: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:13.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:13.2: ‘If only the Teacher, after repeatedly asking me, would teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus*, and not after repeatedly asking some other *bhikkhu* would the Teacher teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus*.’
mn5:13.3: But it is possible, friend, that after repeatedly asking some other *bhikkhu*, the Teacher would teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus*, and not after repeatedly asking that *bhikkhu* would the Teacher teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus*.
mn5:13.4: ‘After repeatedly asking some other *bhikkhu*, the Teacher teaches *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus*; after repeatedly asking me, the Teacher does not teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus*’—
mn5:13.5: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:13.6: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:13.7: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:14.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:14.2: ‘If only the *bhikkhus*, with me at the head again and again, would enter the village for alms, and not with some other *bhikkhu* at the head again and again would the *bhikkhus* enter the village for alms.’
mn5:14.3: But it is possible, friend, that with some other *bhikkhu* at the head again and again, the *bhikkhus* would enter the village for alms, and not with that *bhikkhu* at the head again and again would the *bhikkhus* enter the village for alms.
mn5:14.4: ‘With some other *bhikkhu* at the head again and again, the *bhikkhus* enter the village for alms; with me at the head again and again, the *bhikkhus* do not enter the village for alms’—
mn5:14.5: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:14.6: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:14.7: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:15.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:15.2: ‘If only I myself would receive at the meal the best seat, the best water, the best morsel, and not some other *bhikkhu* would receive at the meal the best seat, the best water, the best morsel.’
mn5:15.3: But it is possible, friend, that some other *bhikkhu* would receive at the meal the best seat, the best water, the best morsel, and that *bhikkhu* would not receive at the meal the best seat, the best water, the best morsel.
mn5:15.4: ‘Some other *bhikkhu* receives at the meal the best seat, the best water, the best morsel; I do not receive at the meal the best seat, the best water, the best morsel’—
mn5:15.5: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:15.6: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:15.7: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:16.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:16.2: ‘If only after eating at the meal I myself would give *anumodanā*, and not some other *bhikkhu*, after eating at the meal, would give *anumodanā*.’
mn5:16.3: But it is possible, friend, that some other *bhikkhu*, after eating at the meal, would give *anumodanā*, and that *bhikkhu*, after eating at the meal, would not give *anumodanā*.
mn5:16.4: ‘Some other *bhikkhu*, after eating at the meal, gives *anumodanā*; I, after eating at the meal, do not give *anumodanā*’—
mn5:16.5: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:16.6: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:16.7: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:17.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:17.2: ‘If only I myself would teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus* who have come to the monastery, and not some other *bhikkhu* would teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus* who have come to the monastery.’
mn5:17.3: But it is possible, friend, that some other *bhikkhu* would teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus* who have come to the monastery, and that *bhikkhu* would not teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus* who have come to the monastery.
mn5:17.4: ‘Some other *bhikkhu* teaches *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus* who have come to the monastery; I do not teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhus* who have come to the monastery’—
mn5:17.5: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:17.6: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:17.7: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:18-20.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:18-20.2: ‘If only I myself would teach *dhamma* to the *bhikkhunis* who have come to the monastery …
mn5:18-20.3: would teach *dhamma* to the male lay followers …
mn5:18-20.4: would teach *dhamma* to the female lay followers who have come to the monastery, and not some other *bhikkhu* would teach *dhamma* to the female lay followers who have come to the monastery.’
mn5:18-20.5: But it is possible, friend, that some other *bhikkhu* would teach *dhamma* to the female lay followers who have come to the monastery, and that *bhikkhu* would not teach *dhamma* to the female lay followers who have come to the monastery.
mn5:18-20.6: ‘Some other *bhikkhu* teaches *dhamma* to the female lay followers who have come to the monastery; I do not teach *dhamma* to the female lay followers who have come to the monastery’—
mn5:18-20.7: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:18-20.8: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:18-20.9: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:2.1: “Friends, these four kinds of persons are found in the world.
mn5:2.10: Now, friends, among these two unblemished persons, the one who, though unblemished, does understand as it truly is, ‘There is no inner blemish in me’—this one is called the superior person.”
mn5:2.3: Here, friends, one person, though blemished, does not understand as it truly is: ‘There is an inner blemish in me.’
mn5:2.4: And here, friends, one person, though blemished, does understand as it truly is: ‘There is an inner blemish in me.’
mn5:2.5: Here, friends, one person, though unblemished, does not understand as it truly is: ‘There is no inner blemish in me.’
mn5:2.6: And here, friends, one person, though unblemished, does understand as it truly is: ‘There is no inner blemish in me.’
mn5:2.7: Now, friends, among these two blemished persons, the one who, though blemished, does not understand as it truly is, ‘There is an inner blemish in me’—this one is called the inferior person.
mn5:2.8: Now, friends, among these two blemished persons, the one who, though blemished, does understand as it truly is, ‘There is an inner blemish in me’—this one is called the superior person.
mn5:2.9: Now, friends, among these two unblemished persons, the one who, though unblemished, does not understand as it truly is, ‘There is no inner blemish in me’—this one is called the inferior person.
mn5:21.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:21.2: ‘If only the *bhikkhus* would honor me, respect me, esteem me, and venerate me, and not honor, respect, esteem, and venerate some other *bhikkhu*.’
mn5:21.3: But it is possible, friend, that the *bhikkhus* would honor, respect, esteem, and venerate some other *bhikkhu*, and would not honor, respect, esteem, and venerate that *bhikkhu*.
mn5:21.4: ‘The *bhikkhus* honor, respect, esteem, and venerate some other *bhikkhu*; they do not honor, respect, esteem, and venerate me’—
mn5:21.5: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:21.6: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:21.7: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:22-24.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:22-24.4: the female lay followers would honor, respect, esteem, and venerate me, and not honor, respect, esteem, and venerate some other *bhikkhu*.’
mn5:22-24.5: But it is possible, friend, that the female lay followers would honor, respect, esteem, and venerate some other *bhikkhu*, and would not honor, respect, esteem, and venerate that *bhikkhu*.
mn5:22-24.6: ‘The female lay followers honor, respect, esteem, and venerate some other *bhikkhu*; they do not honor, respect, esteem, and venerate me’—
mn5:22-24.7: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:22-24.8: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:22-24.9: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:25.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:25.2: ‘If only I myself would be the recipient of fine robes, and not some other *bhikkhu* would be the recipient of fine robes.’
mn5:25.3: But it is possible, friend, that some other *bhikkhu* would be the recipient of fine robes, and that *bhikkhu* would not be the recipient of fine robes.
mn5:25.4: ‘Some other *bhikkhu* is the recipient of fine robes; I am not the recipient of fine robes’—
mn5:25.5: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:25.6: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:25.7: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:26-27.1: It is possible, friend, that here a certain *bhikkhu* might have this wish arise:
mn5:26-27.2: ‘If only I myself would be the recipient of fine almsfood …
mn5:26-27.4: fine medicinal requisites for the sick, and not some other *bhikkhu* would be the recipient of fine medicinal requisites for the sick.’
mn5:26-27.5: But it is possible, friend, that some other *bhikkhu* would be the recipient of fine medicinal requisites for the sick, and that *bhikkhu* would not be the recipient of fine medicinal requisites for the sick.
mn5:26-27.6: ‘Some other *bhikkhu* is the recipient of fine medicinal requisites for the sick; I am not the recipient of fine medicinal requisites for the sick’—
mn5:26-27.7: then he becomes angry and displeased.
mn5:26-27.8: And whatever anger there is, friend, and whatever displeasure there is—
mn5:26-27.9: both of these are a blemish.
mn5:28.1: ‘Blemish,’ friends, is a designation for these bad, unskillful, desire-driven states.
mn5:29.1: Friends, if for any *bhikkhu* these bad, unskillful, desire-driven states are seen and heard to be unabandoned, then even if he is a forest-dweller, one who dwells in remote lodgings, an eater of almsfood, one who goes from house to house in order, a wearer of rag-robes, a wearer of coarse robes, still his fellow practitioners of the holy life neither honor him nor respect him nor esteem him nor venerate him.
mn5:29.10: Even those who were hungry would have no wish to eat, let alone those who were full.
mn5:29.11: Just so, friends, if for any *bhikkhu* these bad, unskillful, desire-driven states are seen and heard to be unabandoned, then even if he is a forest-dweller, one who dwells in remote lodgings, an eater of almsfood, one who goes from house to house in order, a wearer of rag-robes, a wearer of coarse robes, still his fellow practitioners of the holy life neither honor him nor respect him nor esteem him nor venerate him.
mn5:29.13: Because those bad, unskillful, desire-driven states of that venerable one are seen and heard to be unabandoned.
mn5:29.3: Because those bad, unskillful, desire-driven states of that venerable one are seen and heard to be unabandoned.
mn5:29.4: Suppose, friends, a bronze bowl were brought from a shop or from a smith’s workshop, pure and bright.
mn5:29.5: Its owners, placing in it the carcass of a snake, or the carcass of a dog, or the carcass of a human being, would cover it with another bronze bowl and carry it through the market.
mn5:29.7: ‘Hey, what is this that is being carried along as though it were something choice?’
mn5:29.8: Then they would go up to it, uncover it, and look.
mn5:29.9: At the mere sight of it, unpleasantness would arise, revulsion would arise, and disgust would arise.
mn5:3.1: When this was said, the Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said to the Venerable Sāriputta:
mn5:3.2: “What, friend Sāriputta, is the cause, what is the condition, why among these two blemished persons one is called the inferior person and one is called the superior person?
mn5:3.3: And what, friend Sāriputta, is the cause, what is the condition, why among these two unblemished persons one is called the inferior person and one is called the superior person?”
mn5:30.1: Friends, if for any *bhikkhu* these bad, unskillful, desire-driven states are seen and heard to be abandoned, then even if he dwells near a village, accepts invitations, and wears robes from householders, still his fellow practitioners of the holy life honor him, respect him, esteem him, and venerate him.
mn5:30.10: Even those who were full would wish to eat, let alone those who were hungry.
mn5:30.11: Just so, friends, if for any *bhikkhu* these bad, unskillful, desire-driven states are seen and heard to be abandoned, then even if he dwells near a village, accepts invitations, and wears robes from householders, still his fellow practitioners of the holy life honor him, respect him, esteem him, and venerate him.
mn5:30.13: Because those bad, unskillful, desire-driven states of that venerable one are seen and heard to be abandoned.”
mn5:30.3: Because those bad, unskillful, desire-driven states of that venerable one are seen and heard to be abandoned.
mn5:30.4: Suppose, friends, a bronze bowl were brought from a shop or from a smith’s workshop, pure and bright.
mn5:30.5: Its owners, placing in it cooked rice of fine grain, cleaned of black specks, with many soups and many curries, would cover it with another bronze bowl and carry it through the market.
mn5:30.7: ‘Hey, what is this that is being carried along as though it were something choice?’
mn5:30.8: Then they would go up to it, uncover it, and look.
mn5:30.9: At the mere sight of it, pleasantness would arise, non-revulsion would arise, and non-disgust would arise.
mn5:31.1: When this was said, the Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said to the Venerable Sāriputta:
mn5:31.10: And just as that thought arose in the mind of Paṇḍuputta the Ājīvaka, the former wheelwright’s son, so Samīti, the wheelwright’s son, planed away from that rim that bend, that crookedness, and that defect.
mn5:31.11: Then Paṇḍuputta the Ājīvaka, the former wheelwright’s son, pleased, uttered a pleased utterance:
mn5:31.12: ‘I suppose he is planing heart to heart, having known the heart.’
mn5:31.2: “An analogy occurs to me, friend Sāriputta.”
mn5:31.3: “Let it occur to you, friend Moggallāna.”
mn5:31.4: “At one time, friend, I was dwelling at Rājagaha, in the Mountain Citadel.
mn5:31.5: Then, friend, in the morning, after dressing and taking my bowl and robe, I entered Rājagaha for alms.
mn5:31.6: Now at that time Samīti, the wheelwright’s son, was shaping a wheel rim.
mn5:31.7: And Paṇḍuputta the Ājīvaka, the former wheelwright’s son, was standing by him.
mn5:31.8: Then, friend, this thought arose in the mind of Paṇḍuputta the Ājīvaka, the former wheelwright’s son:
mn5:31.9: ‘If only this Samīti, the wheelwright’s son, would plane away from this rim this bend, this crookedness, and this defect, then this rim would be rid of bend, rid of crookedness, rid of defect, pure, established in the solid wood.’
mn5:32.1: Just so, friend, as for those persons who are without faith, who have gone forth from the home life into homelessness not out of faith but for the sake of livelihood; deceitful, devious, tricksters, arrogant, haughty, flighty, talkative, with scattered speech; unguarded at the doors of the *indriyas* [*indriyesu aguttadvārā*], not knowing moderation in eating, not devoted to wakefulness, with no regard for the contemplative life, with no keen respect for the training, indulgent, lax, leading the way in decline, having laid down the burden of seclusion [*paviveke*], lazy, with weak energy, with *sati* lost [*muṭṭhassatī*], not fully aware [*asampajānā*], not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitā*], with a distracted mind, with poor discernment [*duppaññā*], dull-witted—for them, the Venerable Sāriputta, by this exposition of *dhamma* [*dhammapariyāyena*], seems to me to be planing heart to heart, having known the heart.
mn5:32.2: But those sons of good family who have gone forth from the home life into homelessness out of faith; not deceitful, not devious, not tricksters, not arrogant, not haughty, not flighty, not talkative, not with scattered speech; guarded at the doors of the *indriyas* [*indriyesu guttadvārā*], knowing moderation in eating, devoted to wakefulness, having regard for the contemplative life, with keen respect for the training, not indulgent, not lax, having laid down the burden of decline, leading the way in seclusion [*paviveke*], with energy aroused, resolute, with *sati* established [*upaṭṭhitassatī*], fully aware [*sampajānā*], endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitā*], their mind gathered [*ekaggacittā*], possessing discernment [*paññavanto*], not dull-witted—having heard this exposition of *dhamma* [*dhammapariyāyaṁ*] from the Venerable Sāriputta, they seem to me to drink it in, they seem to me to chew on it, with speech as well as with mind:
mn5:32.3: ‘It is good, friend, that our fellow practitioner of the holy life, having raised people out of the unskillful, establishes them in the skillful.’
mn5:33.1: Just as, friend, a woman or a man, young, youthful, fond of adornment, having obtained a garland of blue lotuses, or a garland of jasmine, or a garland of atimuttaka flowers, would receive it with both hands and place it on the head, on the crown;
mn5:33.2: just so, friend, those sons of good family who have gone forth from the home life into homelessness out of faith; not deceitful, not devious, not tricksters, not arrogant, not haughty, not flighty, not talkative, not with scattered speech; guarded at the doors of the *indriyas* [*indriyesu guttadvārā*], knowing moderation in eating, devoted to wakefulness, having regard for the contemplative life, with keen respect for the training, not indulgent, not lax, having laid down the burden of decline, leading the way in seclusion [*paviveke*], with energy aroused, resolute, with *sati* established [*upaṭṭhitassatī*], fully aware [*sampajānā*], endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitā*], their mind gathered [*ekaggacittā*], possessing discernment [*paññavanto*], not dull-witted—having heard this exposition of *dhamma* [*dhammapariyāyaṁ*] from the Venerable Sāriputta, they seem to me to drink it in, they seem to me to chew on it, with speech as well as with mind:
mn5:33.3: ‘It is good, friend, that our fellow practitioner of the holy life, having raised people out of the unskillful, establishes them in the skillful.’”
mn5:33.4: Thus those two great ones approved each other’s well-spoken words.
mn5:33.5: The fifth, Without Blemishes, is finished.
mn5:4.1: “Now, friends, as to the person who, though blemished, does not understand as it truly is, ‘There is an inner blemish in me’—this is to be expected of him: he will not arouse desire, he will not make an effort, he will not stir up energy for the abandoning of that blemish.
mn5:4.2: He will die with passion, with ill will, with delusion, blemished, with a defiled mind.
mn5:4.3: Suppose, friends, a bronze bowl were brought from a shop or from a smith’s workshop, covered with dust and dirt.
mn5:4.4: Its owners would neither use it nor clean it, but would throw it down on a dusty road.
mn5:4.5: Then, friends, after some time that bronze bowl would become even more soiled and stained, would it not?”
mn5:4.7: “Just so, friends, as to the person who, though blemished, does not understand as it truly is, ‘There is an inner blemish in me’—this is to be expected of him: he will not arouse desire, he will not make an effort, he will not stir up energy for the abandoning of that blemish.
mn5:4.8: He will die with passion, with ill will, with delusion, blemished, with a defiled mind.
mn5:5.1: Now, friends, as to the person who, though blemished, does understand as it truly is, ‘There is an inner blemish in me’—this is to be expected of him: he will arouse desire, he will make an effort, he will stir up energy for the abandoning of that blemish.
mn5:5.2: He will die without passion, without ill will, without delusion, unblemished, with an undefiled mind.
mn5:5.3: Suppose, friends, a bronze bowl were brought from a shop or from a smith’s workshop, covered with dust and dirt.
mn5:5.4: Its owners would both use it and clean it, and would not throw it down on a dusty road.
mn5:5.5: Then, friends, after some time that bronze bowl would become even purer and brighter, would it not?”
mn5:5.7: “Just so, friends, as to the person who, though blemished, does understand as it truly is, ‘There is an inner blemish in me’—this is to be expected of him: he will arouse desire, he will make an effort, he will stir up energy for the abandoning of that blemish.
mn5:5.8: He will die without passion, without ill will, without delusion, unblemished, with an undefiled mind.
mn5:6.1: Now, friends, as to the person who, though unblemished, does not understand as it truly is, ‘There is no inner blemish in me’—this is to be expected of him: he will attend to an attractive *nimitta* [*subhanimittaṁ*]; because of attending to that attractive *nimitta*, passion will overwhelm his mind.
mn5:6.2: He will die with passion, with ill will, with delusion, blemished, with a defiled mind.
mn5:6.3: Suppose, friends, a bronze bowl were brought from a shop or from a smith’s workshop, pure and bright.
mn5:6.4: Its owners would neither use it nor clean it, but would throw it down on a dusty road.
mn5:6.5: Then, friends, after some time that bronze bowl would become even more soiled and stained, would it not?”
mn5:6.7: “Just so, friends, as to the person who, though unblemished, does not understand as it truly is, ‘There is no inner blemish in me’—this is to be expected of him: he will attend to an attractive *nimitta* [*subhanimittaṁ*]; because of attending to that attractive *nimitta*, passion will overwhelm his mind.
mn5:6.8: He will die with passion, with ill will, with delusion, blemished, with a defiled mind.
mn5:7.1: Now, friends, as to the person who, though unblemished, does understand as it truly is, ‘There is no inner blemish in me’—this is to be expected of him: he will not attend to an attractive *nimitta* [*subhanimittaṁ*]; because of not attending to that attractive *nimitta*, passion will not overwhelm his mind.
mn5:7.2: He will die without passion, without ill will, without delusion, unblemished, with an undefiled mind.
mn5:7.3: Suppose, friends, a bronze bowl were brought from a shop or from a smith’s workshop, pure and bright.
mn5:7.4: Its owners would both use it and clean it, and would not throw it down on a dusty road.
mn5:7.5: Then, friends, after some time that bronze bowl would become even purer and brighter, would it not?”
mn5:7.7: “Just so, friends, as to the person who, though unblemished, does understand as it truly is, ‘There is no inner blemish in me’—this is to be expected of him: he will not attend to an attractive *nimitta* [*subhanimittaṁ*]; because of not attending to that attractive *nimitta*, passion will not overwhelm his mind.
mn5:7.8: He will die without passion, without ill will, without delusion, unblemished, with an undefiled mind.
mn5:8.1: This, friend Moggallāna, is the cause, this is the condition, why among these two blemished persons one is called the inferior person and one is called the superior person.
mn5:8.2: And this, friend Moggallāna, is the cause, this is the condition, why among these two unblemished persons one is called the inferior person and one is called the superior person.”
mn5:9.2: But, friend, what is this a designation for, namely ‘blemish’?”
mn5:9.3: “Friend, ‘blemish’ is a designation for bad, unskillful, desire-driven states.”
